meta.purpose: Format pilot for user sign-off. Facts drawn only from content already verbatim-verified this project (official English syllabus 9.1–9.3, IDPC corpus passages confirmed during question-bank gates).
meta.patch_note: Gap patches 2026-06-04 per open-cards solve — see qa-log.md

cards.front: What are the five components of business location supervision guidelines?
cards.back: Scope; planning; business location audit program and related training; risk identification; audit report and follow-up.
cards.source_quote: "Understand the guidelines for business location supervision, including: • Scope • Planning • Business location audit program and training • Risk identification • Audit report and follow up" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1612

cards.front: An audit finds deficiencies at a branch. What must happen after the audit report?
cards.back: Documented follow-up: findings tracked to corrective action — unresolved findings escalate; they are never withheld from Executives.
cards.source_quote: "Inadequate follow-up procedures to ensure that compliance audit findings are properly tracked and corrective action is taken by a business location" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1652

cards.front: How long must supervision records (e.g., records of branch reviews) be retained?
cards.back: Minimum 7 years from the date the record is created, in durable and accessible form.
cards.source_quote: "in durable and accessible form, for a minimum of seven years from the date the" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 6721

cards.front: Who oversees the Supervisors at a dealer's business locations?
cards.back: Head office: the dealer must supervise its Supervisors (IDPC 3905 head-office oversight), supported by a location audit program.
cards.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must have procedures in place that ensure that Supervisors are properly performing their supervisory functions." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 7935

cards.front: Name the five business-location risk-factor categories in syllabus 9.2.
cards.back: Internal; reputational; business-activity; location size; client-base.
cards.source_quote: "Internal risk factors, including: - Adequacy of resources - Key person reliance - Principal-agent • Reputational risk, including: - Community-specific marketing strategy and sales practices - Number of outside activities - Any internal disciplinary action - Number of complaints" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1621

cards.front: Internal risk factors of a business location include which three items?
cards.back: Adequacy of resources; key-person reliance; principal-agent relationship.
cards.source_quote: "Internal risk factors, including: - Adequacy of resources - Key person reliance - Principal-agent" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1621

cards.front: Reputational risk factors of a business location include which four items?
cards.back: Community-specific marketing strategy and sales practices; number of outside activities; internal disciplinary actions; number of complaints.
cards.source_quote: "Reputational risk, including: - Community-specific marketing strategy and sales practices - Number of outside activities - Any internal disciplinary action - Number of complaints" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1625

cards.front: Business-activity risk at a location is driven by which factors?
cards.back: Exposure inherent to a product/service offered there; the location's lines of business and aggregate portfolio risk; number of trade errors.
cards.source_quote: "Business location’s business activity risk, including: - Exposure inherent to providing a particular product or service at a business location - Business location’s lines of business and the aggregate risk present in its overall portfolio - Number of trading errors" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1630

cards.front: Business location SIZE risk includes which pair of considerations?
cards.back: Risk-weighting; risk assessment and control practices (plus registrant count context).
cards.source_quote: "Business location size risk, including: - Risk-weighting - Risk assessment and control practices" — syllabus-raw.txt lines 1663-1665

cards.front: What is client-base risk at a business location?
cards.back: The risk the location assumes that clients may use it for inappropriate purposes.
cards.source_quote: "Client base risk, business location assumes the risk that clients may use it for inappropriate actions" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1638

cards.front: TRAP: 'Number of complaints' belongs to which 9.2 risk category — internal or reputational?
cards.back: Reputational. Internal is resources/key-person/principal-agent. Exam distractors swap items across the five categories.
cards.source_quote: "Reputational risk, including: - Community-specific marketing strategy and sales practices - Number of outside activities - Any internal disciplinary action - Number of complaints" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1625

cards.front: TRAP: 'Number of trade errors' belongs to which 9.2 risk category?
cards.back: Business-activity risk — not size risk. Size risk is risk-weighting and risk assessment/control practices.
cards.source_quote: "Business location’s business activity risk, including: - Exposure inherent to providing a particular product or service at a business location - Business location’s lines of business and the aggregate risk present in its overall portfolio - Number of trading errors" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1630

cards.front: Syllabus 9.3 retail distribution risks — name the product-related ones.
cards.back: Sale of complex or opaque products (incl. illiquid/alternative); new or complex products retail investors don't understand.
cards.source_quote: "The sale of complex or opaque products by individual registrants, including illiquid or alternative products" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1643

cards.front: Which books-and-records failure does 9.3 single out for particular attention?
cards.back: Inadequate control over books and records, with particular attention to poor control of client address changes.
cards.source_quote: "Inadequate control over books and records, with particular attention to poor control of client address changes" — syllabus-raw.txt (Element 9, 9.3 parameters)

cards.front: Which supervision-and-control failures does 9.3 flag for retail distribution?
cards.back: Inadequate control/review of advertising and sales literature; lack of documented evidence of overall supervision.
cards.source_quote: "Failure to meet regulatory standards for supervision and control, including inadequate control and review of advertising and sales literature and the lack of documented evidence of overall supervision" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1647

cards.front: Beyond products and supervision controls, list four more 9.3 retail distribution risks.
cards.back: Client margin management failures; weak follow-up on compliance audit findings; improper reassignment of terminated registrants' accounts; commission-based compensation conflicts.
cards.source_quote: "Failure to meet requirements for managing the margin activities of clients • Inadequate follow-up procedures to ensure that compliance audit findings are properly tracked and corrective action is taken by a business location" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1650

cards.front: A branch repeatedly ignores compliance audit findings. What does 9.3 require?
cards.back: Adequate follow-up procedures: findings tracked and corrective action taken by the business location — non-remediation is itself the named risk.
cards.source_quote: "Inadequate follow-up procedures to ensure that compliance audit findings are properly tracked and corrective action is taken by a business location" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1652

cards.front: TRAP: an AP fails 'regulatory, contractual and legal obligations and the duty of integrity.' Which element/outcome tests this?
cards.back: 9.3 (retail distribution risk framing) — distinct from Element 1 ethics outcomes, which test the conduct standards themselves.
cards.source_quote: "Approved Person’s failure to meet regulatory, contractual and legal obligations and to apply high levels of integrity" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1656

cards.front: Which factors make up a business location's risk profile for supervision purposes?
cards.back: Supervisor experience; personnel tenure/turnover; disciplinary/regulatory history; revenue sources; number of client accounts; share of revenue from higher-risk investments; prior compliance audit findings.
cards.source_quote: "consider the risk profile(s) of relevant business locations (Supervisor experience level; tenure/turnover of personnel; internal/external disciplinary history or regulatory actions; revenue sources; number of client accounts; percentage of revenue from higher-risk investments" — research/02 Part 4, Appendix 16 C3-VIII

cards.front: Who must manage and control the specific risks identified at business locations?
cards.back: Qualified individuals with sufficient resources, assigned to those risks — with red flags identified, followed up and escalated.
cards.source_quote: "ensure management and control of specific risks are assigned to qualified individuals with sufficient resources; identify red flags, follow up and escalate" — 02-syllabus-study-guide-competency-profile.md ~line 349

cards.front: Key-person reliance as a location risk means what?
cards.back: The location's supervision or operations depend on one individual — their absence or departure breaks controls.
cards.qa_note: Killed 2026-06-04: bare term 'key person reliance' has no definitional support in any local source; no-quote-no-card policy.

cards.front: TRAP: does a principal-agent arrangement reduce the dealer's supervisory obligations at that location?
cards.back: No — obligations are unchanged; the arrangement is itself an internal risk factor to supervise (see also Rule 2300).
cards.source_quote: "The Dealer Member must treat each of its agents as employees with respect to:" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 1727

cards.front: What belongs in the business location audit program besides the audits themselves?
cards.back: Related training for those conducting audits, defined scope, and planning.
cards.source_quote: "Scope • Planning • Business location audit program and training • Risk identification • Audit report and follow up" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1613

cards.front: Advisory services vs fully discretionary managed products under 9.3 — why are both named?
cards.back: Both are retail distribution channels whose risks (books/records control, supervision standards, margin, documentation) the Supervisor must apply requirements to.
cards.source_quote: "Apply supervisory requirements to retail distribution risks, including advisory services and fully discretionary managed products to specific situations relevant to a Supervisor." — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1640

cards.front: IDPC 1201: when does a RESIDENCE count as a 'business location'?
cards.back: When regular and ongoing activity requiring registration or approval is carried out from it — or when records relating to such activity are kept there.
cards.source_quote: "an activity that requires registration or Corporation approval is carried out by or on behalf of a Dealer Member, and includes a residence if regular and ongoing activity that requires registration or approval is carried out from the residence" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 232

cards.front: IDPC 2202: what must a dealer do when it opens or closes a business location?
cards.back: Notify the Corporation of the opening or closing, under sub-clause 2803(2)(i)(g) — an NRD submission within the time period prescribed by NI 33-109.
cards.source_quote: "Under sub-clause 2803(2)(i)(g), a Dealer Member must notify the Corporation of the opening or closing of a business location." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 1305

cards.front: Which NRD form notifies a change of address, type of location, or supervision of a business location?
cards.back: Form 33-109F3 — Business locations other than head office (same form used for opening/closing notifications under section 2202).
cards.source_quote: "a notification of change of address, type of Form 33-109F3 - Business locations other than location or supervision of any business head office location" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 3784 (two-column table row, flattened)

cards.front: Rule 2200 Part D: what makes a business location a branch office MEMBER of a CIRO Region?
cards.back: Being in a Region with a Supervisor who is normally present at that business location — every such location is a branch office member of the Region.
cards.source_quote: "Every Dealer Member’s business location in a Region with a Supervisor, who is normally present at the business location, is a branch office member of the Region." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 1612

cards.front: GN-3900-20-001: what is the stated DESIGN purpose of second-tier supervision?
cards.back: Ensure no serious account issues were missed by first-tier supervision, and ensure first-tier supervision is conducted adequately. (First tier: resident Supervisors at their location; second tier: normally head office.)
cards.source_quote: "Dealers should design their second-tier supervision to: ensure no serious account issues were missed by ﬁrst-tier supervision, and ensure ﬁrst-tier supervision is conducted adequately." — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 263

cards.front: GN-3900-20-001: second-tier reviewers spot issues they have no authority to resolve. What must exist?
cards.back: Procedures for referring those issues to a Supervisor who has the appropriate authority.
cards.source_quote: "Where the second-tier reviews are conducted by personnel who do not have the authority to resolve the issues they identify, the Dealer should have procedures for referring these issues to a Supervisor who has the appropriate authority." — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 269

cards.front: IDPC 3219(3): who must control and investigate returned retail-client mail?
cards.back: A person independent of the sales function (may sit within a business location); a record of all investigations and results must be maintained.
cards.source_quote: "A Dealer Member’s returned mail procedures for retail clients must at a minimum include the following provisions: (i) a rule requiring the control and investigation by a person independent of the sales function" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4895
